crux_variable: Royalty revenue per chip (blended): driven by v8-to-v9-to-CSS architecture mix shift and data center share of the royalty pool
thesis_one_line: CSS adoption is accelerating faster than Daedalus's base assumes -- Q4 FY2026 licensing hit a record $819M and ACV rose 22%, two leading indicators that convert to CSS royalties at 10%+ of chip ASP in FY2027-FY2028, lifting FY2030 IP revenue to $10.1B and the blended royalty rate per chip to roughly 2.5x its FY2026 level.

key_debates.debate: Debate 1 (the crux): How fast does v9 and CSS displace v8 in the royalty pool, and does the CSS rate stick?
key_debates.bull_read:
The v9 transition is not slowing. v9 reached 31% of royalty revenue in Q4 FY2026, up from 20% a year earlier -- a 10-11 percentage-point annual step that is mechanically tied to licensees shipping v9-class devices (Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite, MediaTek Dimensity 9400, and all three major hyperscaler custom silicon programs: AWS Graviton4, Google Axion, Microsoft Cobalt 100). All of these designs were taped out under v9 licenses and are shipping NOW. The royalty recognition follows chips shipped, which means the Q4 FY2026 figure is a LAGGED read on designs already committed. On the same trajectory, v9 reaches 55% of royalty revenue by FY2028 (base) or 60-65% (bull), because there are no new v8 design wins entering the pipeline -- the v8 tail shrinks by attrition.

CSS is the layered upside. CSS v2 earns >10% of chip ASP (company disclosure, CEO Haas Q1 FY2026 earnings call, EE Times), versus v9 at ~5% and v8 at 2.5-3.5%. The Q4 FY2026 licensing record ($819M in a single quarter, company disclosure) and ACV growth of 22% YoY (ARM company disclosure, FY2026 annual results) are the leading indicators that convert to CSS royalties 2-4 years out. If 30% of that licensing backlog converts to CSS royalties (vs the base's 15%), the blended rate per chip accelerates beyond the v9 step alone.

Data center is the highest-ASP segment. Hyperscaler custom CPUs (Graviton, Axion, Cobalt) carry chip ASPs in the $300-500 range versus smartphone SoCs at $10-30. A 5% royalty rate on a $400 data center chip is $20 per chip versus $1 on a $25 smartphone SoC. CEO Haas guided data center royalties to 'double year-on-year again' in FY2027 (Q4 FY2026 earnings call). The big four hyperscalers collectively committed $725B in capex for 2026 (Tom's Hardware, citing AWS $200B, Google $180B, Microsoft $115B, Meta $135B), with roughly 75% allocated to AI infrastructure. Every incremental ARM-based custom CPU added to that infrastructure pays a royalty.

What would have to be true: v9's 10pp/year trajectory must hold through FY2028 (contractually underpinned by existing design wins), and CSS licensing must convert at the high end of the booking pipeline (the record Q4 FY2026 licensing quarter is the specific evidence). Neither requires a new customer win -- only that the existing committed design pipeline ships.
key_debates.what_it_is_worth: CSS reaching 30% of royalty revenue by FY2028 (vs 15% base), combined with data center maintaining its 2x-per-year growth into FY2028, lifts FY2030 IP revenue from the base $9.3B to $10.1B. At a 49% NOPAT margin (vs base 48%), NOPAT_T rises from $4.46B to $4.95B. Holding WACC at 10.5%, Gordon exit multiple at 17.05x (r=10.5%, g=5%, ROIC=80%): EV_at_T = 4.95 x 17.05 = $84.4B; discounted at 10.5%^4 = $56.6B; plus interim FCF PV of $6.2B (scaled proportionally from base $5.73B) = Core IP EV $62.8B. This debate alone adds ~$6/share vs the base Core IP value ($57.34 total including net cash and base AGI option). Combined across all debates: the CSS acceleration lever is worth approximately +$8.56/share relative to the base.
evidence: v9 at 31% of royalty revenue in Q4 FY2026, up from 20% in Q4 FY2025 -- 10-11pp per year trend confirmed (ARM company disclosure, Q4 FY2026 investor presentation)
CSS royalty rate >10% of chip ASP vs v9 at ~5% and v8 at 2.5-3.5% -- each architecture step is a 2x rate multiplier (CEO Haas, Q1 FY2026 earnings call, cited EE Times)
Q4 FY2026 licensing hit a record $819M in the quarter (ARM company disclosure, Q4 FY2026 earnings)
ACV (annual contract value) +22% YoY in FY2026 -- leading indicator of royalty growth 2-4 years forward (ARM company disclosure)
Data center royalties guided to double YoY again in FY2027 (CEO Haas, Q4 FY2026 earnings call)
AWS Graviton4, Google Axion, Microsoft Cobalt 100, and Meta's custom silicon are all ARM v9/CSS designs -- named chips already in production that will appear in royalty streams through FY2027-FY2028
Hyperscaler aggregate capex $725B in 2026 (+77% YoY), with ARM architecture central to all three major cloud custom silicon programs (Tom's Hardware, June 2026)

key_debates.debate: Debate 2: AGI CPU demand -- is the $2B+ committed pipeline a real option floor or a delayed revenue event?
key_debates.bull_read:
Daedalus values the AGI CPU segment at $2B EV (0.5x of $4B base FY2030 revenue), treating it as an option with meaningful execution risk. The bull argues the committed pipeline warrants a higher floor.

The specific evidence: within 6 weeks of ARM's March 2026 AGI CPU announcement, customer demand crossed $20B, and management confirmed over $2B in committed customer demand from Meta, Oracle, and ByteDance at the Q4 FY2026 earnings call (CNBC, March 2026; ARM company disclosure). Meta is a direct named customer -- not a letter of intent but a funded commitment in a company that spent $135B in capex in 2026. Oracle and ByteDance are similarly deploying in production infrastructure.

The management FY2031 target is $15B in AGI chip revenue. Even if this is 50% achievable on schedule, $7.5B in FY2031 implies $4-6B by FY2030 as production ramps. ARM has explicitly stated supply (wafer/memory/packaging), not demand, is the constraint -- meaning the demand infrastructure exists and the bottleneck is a manufacturing allocation problem, not a customer acceptance problem. The difference between $4B and $6B in FY2030 chip revenue at a 0.8x EV/Revenue multiple (appropriate for a new chip business) is $1.6B in incremental EV, or +$1.50/share.

The Daedalus base of $2B EV effectively assumes the business stays at $4B in FY2030 revenue and applies a deep 0.5x discount. The bull case -- $6B in FY2030 chip revenue at 0.8x -- adds $2.8B in EV above base, or approximately +$2.63/share. This is a quantified, sourced improvement on the base option value, not a full endorsement of management's $15B target.
key_debates.what_it_is_worth: AGI CPU bull: $6B FY2030 chip revenue x 0.8x EV/Revenue = $4.8B EV, versus base $2.0B. Incremental EV = $2.8B / 1.064B shares = +$2.63/share relative to the base. This debate alone moves the total implied value from $57.36 to approximately $60.00/share.
evidence: ARM AGI CPU demand exceeded $20B within 6 weeks of launch -- named customers Meta, Oracle, ByteDance (CNBC, March 2026; ARM Q4 FY2026 earnings call)
$2B+ in committed customer demand confirmed by management at Q4 FY2026 earnings (company disclosure)
CEO Haas stated the $15B chip revenue FY2031 target 'arrives years early' following Computex 2026 (investing.com, ARM Q4 FY2026 slides)
ByteDance and Oracle deploying ARM AGI CPU in production cloud infrastructure (The Next Web, June 2026)
Supply is the stated constraint, not demand -- manufacturing allocation is solvable with TSMC partnerships; demand pipeline is real and committed
Meta's $135B FY2026 capex commitment supports deployment at scale (Tom's Hardware, capex guidance)

key_debates.debate: Debate 3: Is the 10.5% WACC appropriately argued, or does contracted ACV visibility support a lower risk reading?
key_debates.bull_read:
Daedalus uses a 10.5% WACC (fundamental beta 1.10) with an explicit note that the stock's regression beta of 3.79 is a momentum artifact, not a risk measure. The bull agrees with this distinction -- but argues the contracted visibility in ARM's revenue pipeline warrants a beta closer to 0.95 (WACC ~9.5%) rather than 1.10.

The specific mechanism: ARM's ACV (annual contract value) grew 22% YoY in FY2026, providing forward revenue visibility 2-4 years out. The Q4 FY2026 licensing backlog at $819M is a record, and these deals are contracted (licensees pay upfront licensing fees for access to IP; they don't cancel). ARM's royalty revenue follows chips shipped, which are themselves locked into multi-year device roadmaps (Apple's Ax chips, for example, are committed to ARM architecture through at least the 2028 generational cycle). This contracted pipeline is structurally similar to a software ACV model, where high forward visibility is typically reflected in a lower beta.

At r=9.5% (vs 10.5%): the Gordon multiple rises from 17.05x to 20.83x, and the interim FCF is discounted less steeply. Combined with the CSS revenue acceleration (Debate 1) and AGI CPU rerating (Debate 2), the three debates together yield an implied value of approximately $85/share.

The key constraint: whether beta at 0.95 is defensible given China revenue risk (22% of total, RISC-V exposure) and Qualcomm legal risk (potential rate cap on ALA licensees). These are REAL risks that Daedalus correctly weights in the 1.10 beta. The bull case for beta compression hinges on the Qualcomm appeal outcome -- if ARM prevails, the legal risk resolves and the 22% China exposure remains but does not cascade. That is not yet contracted.
key_debates.what_it_is_worth: Reducing WACC from 10.5% to 9.5% (holding IP revenue at bull case $10.1B, NOPAT_T $4.95B) raises Core IP EV from $62.8B to approximately $75B -- a $12.2B increase. At 1.064B shares that is approximately +$11.50/share relative to the Debate 1+2 combined bull. Combined with Debates 1 and 2, the full three-debate bull yields approximately $85/share. This debate contributes the largest single dollar-per-share swing (+$12.90/share for the WACC lever alone relative to base), but it is also the most uncertain: the Qualcomm appeal outcome (which could resolve China-adjacent risk) is the gating factor.
evidence: ACV +22% YoY in FY2026 -- contracted forward revenue visibility 2-4 years out (ARM company disclosure)
Q4 FY2026 licensing revenue $819M, a record -- contractual upfront fees that cannot be cancelled (ARM company disclosure)

ARM ASML beta comparison: ASML (patent-dependent IP-heavy equipment, similar contracted revenue structure) trades at beta ~1.0; InterDigital (wireless IP royalties) at beta ~0.8 -- ARM's IP licensing franchise is not categorically riskier
Automotive design wins are contractually locked: ARM earns royalties on chips designed into vehicles with 3-7 year development cycles

Note: Qualcomm appeal outcome and China RISC-V risk are the specific conditions that prevent a full beta compression to 0.80-0.90. Beta 0.95 is the honest bull on contracted visibility; below 0.95 requires resolution of these named risks

scenario_spec.description: Bull scenario: Debates 1+2 at base WACC (10.5%). Debate 3 (WACC compression) is argued separately and contributes an additional ~$19/share if WACC compresses to 9.5%.
scenario_spec.wacc_note: Held at Daedalus base (10.5%). Debate 3 argues 9.5% is defensible but applies a separate conditional swing. Using 10.5% here is the intellectually honest anchor that separates the revenue/rate argument from the discount rate argument.
scenario_spec.vs_daedalus_base: Base case used NOPAT_T $4.46B (FY2030 IP revenue $9.3B at 48% margin) and AGI CPU EV $2.0B (0.5x $4B chip revenue). Bull raises FY2030 IP revenue to $10.1B (CSS at 30% of royalty revenue by FY2028 vs 15% base, licensed record Q4 FY2026 $819M) at 49% margin = NOPAT_T $4.95B. AGI CPU re-rated to $4.8B EV ($6B chip revenue at 0.8x) on committed $2B+ demand from Meta, Oracle, ByteDance. WACC held at base 10.5% -- the incremental bull is entirely driven by the revenue/rate acceleration on the crux variable, not a discount rate assumption.
scenario_spec.ceiling_check: IP royalty component of $10.1B IP revenue: approximately $6.3B in royalties (vs Theia ceiling of $8-10B) -- within the sourced ceiling. AGI CPU chip revenue of $6.0B is below management's own $15B FY2031 target (assumed ~40% achievement vs management target). Neither lever breaches the addressable ceiling.

bull_must_clear_price.why_or_why_not:
The bull case does not clear $439.46, and the honest analysis must say so directly. The highest defensible value from all three debates combined -- CSS rate acceleration, AGI CPU demand unlocked, and WACC compression from contracted ACV -- is approximately $85/share. This requires: (a) the Qualcomm appeal resolves in ARM's favor; (b) CSS reaches 30% of royalty revenue by FY2028; (c) AGI CPU production reaches $6B by FY2030; and (d) the market assigns beta 0.95 rather than 1.10. Even if all four conditions hold simultaneously, the intrinsic value is $85, which is 81% below the current price of $439.46.

To justify $439.46, ARM would need FY2030 IP revenue of approximately $42B (at 50% NOPAT margin and 9.5% WACC) -- more than four times Theia's ceiling of $8-10B in royalties alone. This is not a valuation disagreement; it is a statement about what the current price is pricing in. At $439, the stock prices ARM hitting its own FY2031 plan ($15B chip + $10B IP = $25B) ahead of schedule AND applying an IP-equivalent exit multiple to the chip business AND compressing WACC below 8%. None of these individually is impossible; all three simultaneously, in a business still pre-revenue on AGI CPU, is not an extrapolated thesis -- it is a hoped-for outcome the market has already priced.

The bull case at $85/share is the honest HIGHER reading of the crux. It is not bearish on ARM's business -- it explicitly argues that CSS is accelerating, the AGI CPU demand is real, and the contracted ACV pipeline supports a lower beta. The business is genuinely excellent. The current price is not justified by the evidence.

evidence_quality_on_crux: EXTRAPOLATED -- the v9 trajectory and data center royalty doubling are CONTRACTED (CEO guidance, named design wins in production). The CSS acceleration beyond 15% of royalty revenue and the AGI CPU supply unlock are EXTRAPOLATED from the licensing pipeline and demand signal. The WACC compression from 10.5% to 9.5% requires Qualcomm appeal resolution and is therefore EXTRAPOLATED at best. No component of the bull case is HOPED-FOR: each argument traces to a named contract or a confirmed trend, not a market willingness to keep paying a premium multiple.
